title: AMD EPYC 8005 Sorano Completely Changes AMD SP6
gist: AMD's new EPYC 8005 'Sorano' keeps the same SP6 socket as the EPYC 8004 'Siena' but switches from Zen 4c to full Zen 5 cores, pushing core counts to 84 and closing the architectural gap between AMD's low-power edge/telco line and its flagship EPYC 9005 'Turin' server line, at a power-efficiency cost of under 10% at the system level.

insights.claim: AMD's EPYC 8005 'Sorano' is a drop-in replacement for the EPYC 8004 'Siena' in the same SP6 socket, requiring only a BIOS update on supporting platforms, while raising core counts from a lower ceiling up to 84 cores/168 threads and 384MB of L3 cache.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Server OEMs and buyers on the SP6/Siena platform get a free performance upgrade path without a hardware redesign, which shortens replacement cycles and could pressure competing edge/telco CPU platforms (Intel Xeon-D-class parts) on price/performance.
insights.quote: The new EPYC 8005 series is slated as a drop-in replacement (with BIOS update if the platform supports it) to the EPYC 8004 “Siena” series.

insights.claim: The EPYC 8005 series switches from Zen 4c cores (used in EPYC 8004) to full Zen 5 cores, the same core architecture and full L3 cache design used in AMD's EPYC 4005 desktop-derived line and the flagship EPYC 9005 'Turin' series (excluding Turin Dense).
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: AMD is unifying its core IP across product tiers (edge/SP6, desktop-derived, and flagship server), reducing the need for a distinct compact-core (4c) design in this segment and simplifying AMD's roadmap — a structural signal that AMD may deprioritize density-optimized 'c' cores in lower-tier SKUs going forward.
insights.quote: Now the EPYC 8005 series is using the same full cache Zen 5 cores that the EPYC 4005 series is using, as well as the EPYC 9005 “Turin” series (save the Turin Dense parts).
insights.locator: AMD EPYC 8125P In HPE ProLiant DL145 Gen11 Topology

insights.claim: The author frames the core-architecture unification across the EPYC 8005, EPYC 4005, and EPYC 9005 lines as more than a technical change — it is also a psychological/market-positioning shift, since a customer previously buying a 'lesser' Zen 4c chip in the 8004 series now gets the same core IP as flagship EPYC 9005 parts.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Perception of tiering matters for enterprise procurement decisions; removing the 'compact core' stigma from the SP6 line could pull budget-conscious buyers who previously defaulted to Intel or a higher AMD SKU toward the cheaper SP6 platform.
insights.quote: To me, this is one of those technical changes that will also border on the psychological.
insights.locator: AMD EPYC 8125P In HPE ProLiant DL145 Gen11 Topology

insights.claim: The EPYC 8005 series raises default TDP by 25W (12.5%) over EPYC 8004, but real-world system-level power draw at 100% utilization only increases by roughly 30-34W, under a 10% delta, despite delivering more cores, a newer core architecture, and faster memory.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Datacenter operators evaluating the platform can expect a favorable performance-per-watt gain since the compute uplift (more cores, faster clocks, newer architecture, faster memory) is not proportionally offset by power draw, supporting a TCO case for upgrading SP6 fleets.
insights.quote: While folks may fixate on the 25W higher TDP, at a system level, it ends up being closer to 30-34W higher power consumption at 100% utilization, which is under 10%.

insights.claim: EPYC 8005 memory support increases to DDR5-6400 (from EPYC 8004's slower memory) and adds CXL 2.0 support within the same SP6 package size roughly equivalent to a Threadripper package.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: CXL 2.0 support at this tier signals AMD is pushing memory-pooling/expansion capability down into its lower-cost edge/embedded server segment, which is relevant as DRAM scarcity (per concurrent industry reporting) makes memory-expansion technologies like CXL increasingly valuable even outside flagship server tiers.

insights.claim: AMD's EPYC 8005 SKU stack spans $529 to $5799 across 8 to 84 cores, and the top 84-core SKU is priced only slightly above the 64-core SKU, indicating AMD is optimizing the stack around a $/core metric rather than a flat per-tier price jump.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Buyers needing maximum core density per socket get an outsized deal at the top of the stack, which could accelerate adoption of the highest-core SKUs among density-sensitive customers (cloud/edge hosting) and pressure competitors' per-core pricing in the same segment.
insights.quote: The increase in the top-end price also comes with more cores, so AMD is focusing more on the $/core metric.
insights.locator: AMD EPYC 8005 SKU List And Pricing
